text: The presence of Provincial Reconstruction Teams (PRTs) in Afghanistan has been criticized and contended by many nongovernmental organizations (NGOs) who believe the integration of military personnel into aid work creates “blurred lines” that undermine the security of aid work. The criticism runs along the lines that because the work is carried out by civilians, and because of the frequency of visits and the nature of the work, the insurgency is better able to target aid workers and others who have become more visible because of the integration of civilian and military personnel. Although the phrase is often used as explanation, there has been little analysis to test the notion. This study uses panel corrected standard error regression in all 34 provinces of Afghanistan over the period 2010 and 2011 to assess whether there was an increase in security incidents affecting NGOs in provinces where a PRT is stationed. Preliminary data suggests that there was an increase in the number of incidents in PRT provinces but further investigation points toward a more complex picture. Provinces with a non-US PRT had a higher number of incidents that fell within the margin of error, while provinces with a US-led PRT had incidents fell within the margin of error. This points to the “blurred lines” being exaggerated as a general threat for NGOs and that the way a PRT is carried out is as important if not more important in general than the integration of civilian and military personnel.
headline: Fully Human Written
